title: 28 quotes from next UK leader Andy Burnham on climate, net-zero and fossil fuels - Carbon Brief
author: Carbon Brief Staff
gist: A compiled record of incoming UK PM Andy Burnham's climate statements since 2008 shows a consistently pro-net-zero, pro-fossil-fuel-phase-out track record (including a June 2025 endorsement of the international fossil fuel treaty and a 2015 fracking moratorium call), contrasting sharply with his deliberately vague 'open mind' stance on new North Sea drilling taken during his July 2026 leadership campaign.

themes.theme: Burnham's deliberate ambiguity on North Sea drilling vs. his historical record

insights.claim: As UK prime-minister-designate, Andy Burnham said in June 2026 he has 'something of an open mind' and 'no fixed position' on reversing Labour's manifesto pledge to stop new North Sea oil and gas drilling, a notably vague stance despite the oil-and-gas industry and right-wing media actively lobbying for reversal.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Signals Burnham is deliberately keeping optionality open on North Sea licensing policy ahead of taking office, a key near-term catalyst for UK offshore oil and gas investment decisions.
insights.quote: I've got something of an open mind, you know. I don't have a sort of fixed position.

insights.claim: Just a year before becoming PM, in June 2025 as mayor of Greater Manchester, Burnham endorsed the international fossil fuel treaty -- a proposed pact to phase out coal, oil and gas -- calling it a 'lifeline' and urging 'all governments' to join, and said there 'should be no turning away from net-zero.'
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Establishes a documented recent public commitment to fossil-fuel phase-out that stands in tension with his current 'open mind' stance on North Sea drilling -- a credibility gap investors should watch as he sets actual policy.
insights.quote: I am proud to endorse the fossil-fuel treaty proposal today on behalf of Greater Manchester. It's not just a plan – it's a lifeline. It's a call to end coal, oil and gas, hold polluters accountable…I urge all governments, nationals and subnationals to join this fight.

insights.claim: In 2015, Burnham called for a fracking moratorium citing 'far too many potential risks and unanswered questions,' and in 2019 said 'fracking is the past, it is not the future.'
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A long, consistent anti-fracking record suggests Burnham is unlikely to reverse UK fracking bans even if he allows new conventional North Sea drilling -- useful for differentiating investment exposure across UK fossil-fuel sub-sectors.
insights.quote: Fracking is the past, it is not the future.

insights.claim: Burnham has repeatedly framed net-zero and decarbonisation as an economic opportunity rather than a cost, saying in 2025 that 'an opportunity is opening up for Britain as other countries move away from net-zero,' urging the UK to 'seize that' and become 'a green leader.'
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Suggests Burnham may position UK net-zero policy as a competitive-advantage play versus peers retreating from climate ambition -- relevant for gauging future UK green-industrial-policy support and green finance flows.
insights.quote: An opportunity is opening up for Britain as other countries move away from net-zero. We should seize that…We can make Britain a green leader. This is not the time to tiptoe, it is the time to commit to this path.

insights.claim: In 2021 Burnham warned that net-zero risked becoming the 'next Brexit' -- a debate that could split along class lines if people came to associate the transition with cost and inconvenience for ordinary people while the wealthy avoid the burden through offsetting.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Flags Burnham's early recognition of political risk from unequal net-zero cost distribution -- a signal that his government may prioritize distributionally fair decarbonisation policy design (e.g., subsidized retrofits, targeted bill relief) over blunt carbon pricing.
insights.quote: All of a sudden, you can feel how net-zero could become the new Brexit – a debate that gets very divided on class grounds.

insights.claim: Burnham set a target for Greater Manchester to reach net-zero by 2038 -- 12 years ahead of the UK's national 2050 target -- and championed building 30,000 net-zero social rented homes in the region.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: His regional track record of setting more ambitious decarbonisation timelines than the national target suggests he may push for accelerated national net-zero delivery once in office, relevant to forecasting UK climate policy ambition under his leadership.
insights.quote: [We] need a government that fully buys into the 2038 vision because the UK will not get to 2050 unless places like Greater Manchester are freed up to go faster – and we're ready to go faster.

insights.claim: Burnham has argued that decarbonisation is 'the route to level up the country,' framing it as delivering cheaper public transport, lower-cost homes and thousands of jobs, and called for 'long-term predictable funding' at scale, similar to plans discussed by Rachel Reeves.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Signals Burnham may push for substantial upfront public investment in decarbonisation infrastructure as a 'levelling up' economic tool, a potential driver of UK green-infrastructure capex under his government.
insights.quote: If we really embrace the drive to net-zero, that is the route to level up the country…But it needs substantial investment, upfront, now.

insights.claim: Burnham said in a July 2026 LBC interview that he would lay out a plan for 'more public control over water, energy, transport' to bring down bills and fares and give people and businesses 'breathing space' -- consistent with reports he is preparing to nationalise Thames Water.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Signals a broader interventionist policy direction toward public ownership of utilities under Burnham, relevant for investors assessing regulatory/nationalisation risk in UK water and energy infrastructure assets.
insights.quote: What I would do, if successful, is lay out a plan for more public control over water, energy, transport, so that over the period we can get those bills down, fares down, and give people and give businesses breathing space.

insights.claim: Burnham opposes a third runway at Heathrow but has questioned whether the aviation debate should focus on accelerating research into low- and zero-carbon aviation rather than simply restricting flying.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Suggests a nuanced Burnham stance on aviation -- opposing airport expansion while supporting technology-led decarbonisation of flight -- relevant for assessing UK aviation and sustainable-aviation-fuel policy support.
insights.quote: I don't believe there should be a third runway at Heathrow, for instance.
